affected_groups: currently high-achieving students in elite tracks
content.decision_question: Does admitting more disadvantaged students into existing selective tracks via quotas produce durable educational and labour-market benefit, or does it primarily redistribute reference-group and psychological costs without commensurate gain?
content.evidence_boundary: Further longitudinal and value-added research disentangling institutional effect from family-background selection, and psychological studies on reference-group effects for quota-admitted students specifically, could substantially narrow this uncertainty. However, no amount of such evidence resolves the separate value question of whether formal access itself is worth pursuing even under uncertain benefit — that judgment about the meaning and worth of opportunity remains contested independent of the empirical answer.
content.tension: T5's quota/outreach approach and, to a lesser extent, T3's admissions redesign presume that gaining access to elite tracks benefits disadvantaged students, but evidence on value-added gains being substantially attributable to family background, a possibly zero long-run tertiary/labour-market effect of track selection, and psychological reference-group costs (big-fish-little-pond dynamics) for high-achieving students placed in top-ranked peer groups together raise a genuine empirical and psychological open question about whether such access delivers real benefit or imposes uncompensated social-comparison costs.
content.title: Whether expanding access to selective tracks benefits disadvantaged students or merely relocates them into contested-value settings
value_poles: expanding formal access/opportunity for disadvantaged students
ensuring interventions deliver genuine, not merely symbolic, benefit
